sentences.text: I was born in Uzhorod, Czechoslovakia, 6th of May, 1923.
My parents were in business-- very successful people, very good father-- [SOB] --Very good mother.
I had three brothers and a sister.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One of my brothers went to Israel in 1939.
And he was spared all the problems.
The other two brothers were together.
[SOB] I went to school as a child.
Naturally, I finished elementary school, high school.
Then I went to a special, which was a highly qualified business academy.
In the school, there was about 6,000 students, three Jews-- excuse me, four.
It was a four-year school, which was like a graduate school.
But every year, [SOB] One of the guys had be eliminated.
So second year, only three.
Third year, two.
And graduation was only one.
You had to be real exceptional to get in.
I got in.
And the fourth year, we both went in.
Because after the third year, one of the professors wanted to eliminate me.
Because that was the agreement.
And he said I was cheating on my physics test, which wasn't true.
Principal was a very decent man, Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
[SOB]
Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH] you know, who called me and asked me if I cheated.
Because I did 100% on the test.
So I asked him, well, how was the second best one?
He said 95.
So I told him, how can I cheat, if there were 95 and I did it?
Well, he couldn't get along, but the professor insisted that he wants to throw me out because one has to be eliminated.
And he chose me because he didn't like me.
So I had to take over the test in September, before the fourth year started.
And I got a special test.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I did 98% on the test.
So naturally, I was reinstated and I graduated.
Then before graduation, just about two months, the Germans came in and occupied Uzhorod.
And we were put in a ghetto, a brick manufacturing factory.
We stayed there I would say about four weeks as far as I remember, I don't know exactly.
But one day, I know I had a call from the SS guard that I should-- my name was called, and I should go to the front door, you know, gate, which was closed.
And I went there.
And it was my principal.
[SOB] He took me out.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [CRYING] And he told me that he wants me to finish the graduation.
When I went out, he told me I took you out, because I don't want you to go back.
[CRYING] I told him I can't leave my parents.
I told him then he would be in trouble too.
He said, don't worry about me.
I got permission from the gestapo.
But I always thought-- [CRYING] I always thought if I go back, I can help my parents.
I mean, not that they were that old, you know.
But I figured-- but anyway, it was worthless.
Because I could have escaped, but I didn't do it.
And I'm not sorry.
Because-- [SOB] because you would have to live with that.
Maybe I could have helped, which is not true.
You couldn't do nothing.
And I was there about, what, in the ghetto, what, about four weeks, approximately.
Then they took us to Birkenau-- Auschwitz Birkenau.
I was in the B Lagern.
And I was there-- I was told, oh, what I thought first I had 10 days.
But then I realized more.
It was three weeks, it was three weeks.
I thought at first it was 10 days, but I was thinking in between, and I realize it was three weeks.
Then when was the pick by the SS big commanders to go to work.
They asked for engineers.
So we knew it's a good transport.
This transport meant to France, a French concentration camp-- Longwy.
And we did.
And both of my brothers and me, we went to Longwy-Thil concentration camp, which was on the boarder Belgium and Luxembourg.
We stayed there from-- let me see, from late April 1944 until September 1944.
I don't remember the exact date.
And then as the allies were advancing, they took us.
They put us in cattle car again, took us to Germany to a concentration camp, Kochendorf.
That was a terrible camp, a terrible camp.
They took 500 of us there, in France in the Longwy camp, there was 500 special engineers.
We'd been working there in an underground mine, which was a salt mine converted airplane factory.
I was an electric welder.
The work was 3 o'clock, cell up, we are waking up.
Then they give us a little black coffee.
That's all.
And we marched about-- from the camp to the mine, about an hour, hour and 15 minutes.
We arrived there.
And he worked until about 6, 7 o'clock at night.
Lunch time, there was a break, 15 minutes.
And we received a little black bread.
That's it.
And then at night-- remember, it was a seven day proposition.
There was no break in between.
And we went back then to the camp, where we got a little so-called soup, with a slice of bread, you know what I mean?
I don't know what kind of bread it was.
But it was the best bread we ever ate.
You know, when you're hungry, the best there was.
And that went on.
Over there, the SS who was with us in France, as long as we did work-- and naturally, everybody worked.
They were pretty reasonable.
There were a lot of beatings, a lot of beatings.
But it wasn't atrocious.
When we got to Kochendorf, then we find out what really life is.
And every day, they put us in a group, 100 people in a group.
Very seldom I was with either of my brothers, you know.
Because we didn't have no choice.
He came.
He pointed.
You, you, you, you, and you went.
The SS who made the [GERMAN] SS who made a choice who goes to work, what commander.
The [GERMAN], who was a civilian, a civilian, you know.
He wasn't-- he was also German.
He was from Aachen.
He's the one who made 100-- there was about 10 groups, 100 each, about 1,000 people were there in Kochendorf.
And we were in different details, always in the salt mine, under that salt mine, different was, but the conditions were terrible.
Because the way we understood that, it never came back 100, never.
Most often it was 80.
The rest was all killed or beaten to death or just died from starvation.
We were walking-- marching, you know, from the camp to the working place in the mine.
And we walked through an apple field, you know.
There were millions of apple trees, you know what I mean?
And the trees were on the floor.
Anybody who picked up an apple was shot or beaten to death.
Otherwise, there's no way.
We see it the hard way.
The minute you picked it up, they came, shoot you in the back of your neck.
And that was the end.
They threw you on the side.
Because he had to eliminate so many people anyway everyday.
So he figured this is one way, one process.
But I remember days when there was only 50 returning from the 100, you know, from the morning.
And that was a terrible camp.
I was there from-- the beating was enormous.
Because they were all young SS, you know what I mean?
And mostly, we had a couple from Vienna, Austria.
They are the worst ever.
They want to show they are better than the Germans.
And their atrocities were enormous.
I remember distinctly he always said, [GERMAN] Viennese, like, proud of it, you know what I mean?
But you know, he was worse than the German SS.
Not that other ones were angels, you know what I mean?
But-- and we were there from September, 1944 until about beginning of March, 1945.
Me?
Million times, constantly.
I was beaten once.
That time, it was my older brother Charlie, who survived with me, who died just about seven or eight years ago, here in Los Angeles.
He was with me.
And the beating was so severe that the blood was coming out like-- [CRYING] I put my hand.
And for some miracle, there was, no-- stop.
But we were beaten constantly, constant beating.
I mean constantly.
My younger brother, the beating he got.
[SOBBING] I can't believe it.
A 16-year-old kid, what he endured.
He died the day of liberation in Dachau, the day we were liberated.
But they made a cripple out of him anyway.
He would not be good.
Thank God he died.
Because for some reason, some people got beaten more than other ones.
I don't know why.
I mean, there's no reason in the world.
We did our work, really.
Believe me, there was no choice to do your work now.
Because you know, besides what's-his-name, at the working place, there were no-- there were the kapos, you know, kapos with us.
And they didn't have no choice, but they were beating us.
Because the SS says, go ahead, beat him.
If he didn't, they beat him.
So you know, it was survival of the fittest.
That's all, there's nothing you could do.
And I remember this was in Longwy, yeah, at the beginning.
And an SS told me come sit here.
I didn't know why.
I went there.
And he took out a bayonet or a knife, and he stabbed me here, of across.
No reason.
And they were laughing.
[SOB]
So much fun they had.
I never saw a human being behaving towards another human like this.
Worse than animals, honest.
If it would be a reason-- This was just in France, you know, in Longwy.
And can you imagine, those were the good camps.
Now, in what's the name, in Kochendorf, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were terrible situation.
I mean, the sleeping condition, everything you know.
I didn't have no shoe.
Because the shoe I got an Auschwitz-- number, you know, when they undress you and you got a shoe, it was double my size.
And eventually, I lost it.
I lost it, you know, because it was so big.
You didn't have no choice.
They threw it at you.
I mean, in Birkenau, that's the only shoe I had.
You drink it.
So because everybody was dirty.
And delousing process.
That was the fun for the SS.
And they said anybody who has more than 10 louse-- everybody had a million, not 10, OK-- they will get.
No, no.
This one was in Kochendorf.
Kochendorf.
This one was in Germany already.
No, this was not in Auschwitz, no.
This was Paris, France.
We were lucky.
Because I was about 50th.
And the SS, they just-- they lost their strength.
They could not do it to everybody.
Nothing, nothing.
It was so secretive, everything, that nothing.
If you would have known, I would never be in Auschwitz, none of us.
Nothing.
They told us we're going to a working camp.
And we're going to work.
When the war is over, everybody goes home, and that's it.
So my poor father says, look.
They're not going to kill us, just because we are Jews.
We are not criminals.
We're not even political, you know what I mean?
Not that we are communists or socialists, whatever.
We are just normal people who worked hard all their life.
And they will not kill us, he assured me.
I asked him, what will happen?
He said, don't worry.
We're all going to work.
And the work will be over soon.
And we're going to come home.
So did you see any signs?
Were there any signs of danger or did you have any-- had you any information?
None, none.
That's what I wondered after the war, hindsight, naturally.
How is that possible you didn't have any information?
It was so secretive, everything, that, you know, to come backwards, when they took us away from Uzhorod to Auschwitz, it took us about four days in cattle wagon, actually.
There was 100 people in a cattle-- very hot, no water, no toilet facilities whatsoever.
They gave you a bucket over there, a bucket.
And then they used that, but 100 people, you know what I mean?
There is one bucket.
And number one, the smell was completely choking.
And water was the most thing, you know what I mean.
We had a little food, which we took it from home.
But there was no water.
And I remember it took us, I think, about four days.
In our particular cattle car, there must have been 20 dead, who died during the transportation.
One on my aunts too died, older people, you know?
They died.
And then, you know, they just died.
They didn't say one word.
When they opened the cattle, they told us, one is dead.
That's all.
It was no big deal.
Because they would take them anyway.
That was already in Birkenau.
And that was a separation.
Like I told you, my mother and father went to the right.
And we basically didn't realize.
We were dazed.
We were so thirsty that you didn't realize that was going on.
All you are was [GERMAN].
Nobody give it to us.
But anyway, when we got to separation, they went to the right.
We didn't know.
We thought that older people were going to go to the right, you know?
Like, no, because they're not going to work them.
We're going to work, and we we will there together.
And then we asked the kapos who are in Auschwitz, one, there were human and there were inhuman kapos.
The inhuman always said, oh, you see?
That's where your father is.
Pointed to the chimney.
[CRYING] And the human one, you know what I mean, they just told us don't worry.
They were all kinds of people, the kapos said, don't worry.
You're going to meet them later.
They knew.
But they were human beings.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHING] In Auschwitz, only work we did-- at least I did.
We were taking one pile of stone-- heavy stone, 40, 50 pounds-- taking them to the corner, about 20 feet away.
When that expired, you take from there, put it back.
Just do [INAUDIBLE].
But you see, when we got to Auschwitz, we never was hungry before like this one.
That's where we first got the feeling of the hunger, you know what I mean?
It was bad, very bad.
And that's the type of work we done.
We done it only about four or five hours a day.
Because obviously, they were seeing that we were the work commanders, you know, the ones they knew were going to go out to work, you know what I mean?
Because-- which I did not know then, I know now.
At that time, they needed the forced labor, you know what I mean?
Before, they didn't need to bother with it.
They took everybody, killed them.
And like I told you, my-- what's his name-- further up, [INAUDIBLE], we went to France.
And I know that in Kochendorf, there was terrible things happening-- hanging every day.
There was 1,000 of us in Kochendorf, 1,000.
Because-- the reason I know approximate is because there were 10 groups.
So we realized 100 people to it was.
You know, maybe some were 80.
You know what I mean?
There was a ton of us.

And everybody went out during the day to different type-- in the same salt mine, which was converted to a factory, to different type of work, all very heavy labor, very heavy labor, you know what I mean?
I know-- Can you describe the heavy labor?
I mean, what was it that--
Heavy labor?
OK.
You worked in the mine.
All right, every day, you had a different story.
Some days, you did this one.
And you had to push that little cart which is in a mine.
I'm sure you've seen it, you know what I mean?
And we got salt.
In other part of the mine, they got coal.
We had to load it up with shovel, load it up, push it down on the rail, take them about a mile away, load them into trains, which would carry it away, all right?
End of the mine.
That was no problem.
The problem was, it was too heavy for us.
Four people to a-- that's all, four people pushing.
They were too heavy for us.
And if the car for any reason slipped off the rail, that was sabotage.
They beat you to death, you know what I mean, sabotage.
It happened to me once.
And I was lucky because there was some Italian civilian were over there.
And they right away came, and they put it back.
We could not.
Because before the SS even came, they already help us.
So that saved us from being beaten or even killed, right away.
And then we had to, you know-- in one of the parts, they were manufacturing those rails, the heavy rails, for the railroad, you know?
And-- very heavy.
Six people carrying a rail, which was about 300 kilogram.
This was about 700 pounds, for these type of people, you know what I mean?
So most of the time, I know my poor brother, little brother, he was on it.
I was lucky.
I was only once.
But he on it about three or four times.
That killed him.
Because you can't put it on the shoulder, you know what I mean?
No.
Four people stood here, and four people put them on-- you know, lift it up and put it on your shoulder.
And carry it a mile, that was a test of inhuman.
Naturally, you died.
They beat you to death right there.
And my little brother was completely crippled.
He was better off he died.
Because he was completely bent over from the heavy work.
And the only thing [GERMAN] you know, with the-- you know, they had the whip.
[GERMAN], constantly, [GERMAN], you know what I mean?
And then if the SS didn't feel good and they wanted to have fun, then they took it out on on us.
I mean, took it out on us.
Number one, I remember many times they skipped the lunch, the big lunch, you know what I mean?
Something-- you know, a slice of bread.
They skipped it.
They say, today's no food, which we lived, actually, really for that food.
And as the time went by, the only reason I want to really survive, you know what I mean, that to tell the world-- [SOB] that what happened.
Because I thought I'd be the only one who survives.
Because I seen every day, you know what I mean?
You went into the camp.
Where is this one, where is that one?
Died, killed, you know, shot.
I remember there was a young boy over there from Poland.
He was 15 years old.
He find a potato on the floor, a raw potato.
He picked it up.
The SS seen it.
They hanged him the next day for that.
We were in the mine, and another young man-- I think he was a French Jew, I'm almost sure.
France or Holland, I don't remember.
He tripped on a cord.
That was sabotage, because he pulled it out, the machine stopped.
But he didn't see it.
And there was a public hanging the next day.
You know, he tripped on it.
Yeah, downstairs, you know, in the mine.
They were all over cords.
He didn't realize it.
No, no, you are a saboteur.
You stopped the work for one minute until you plugged it in again.
That's all it was, you know what I mean?
That was that.
And I remember that vividly.
Because I know his father was there.
And he tried to talk to the SS that they should hang him-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [CRYING] instead of the little boy.
And the SS told him, because I was a witness, OK.
You should see I'm not an animal.
I'll let you do it.
But your son has to do it.
[CRYING] Your son has do the hanging.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So they put a noose around the father, put out a stool.
And the son had to kick it.
That was it.
These stories you don't forget.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These things haunt you.
This is not what somebody told me.
This is what I see with my own eye.
You know, there are a lot of stories, which you did not basically see it.
I believed it was true, because there was no story cruel enough it should not be true.
Then-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember in Auschwitz, I was there.
And came in a civilian train, which was very unusual.
And I see nurses with white uniform.
